text: The surgeon initiates an incision on the right side of the upper mesorectum, revealing the loose fibrous layer within the dissectable tissue plane.

The surgeon identifies the left hypogastric nerve and applies ventral traction to the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicel, extending the fibrous tissues behind it. The incision of the peritoneum continues up to the root of the IMA. Dissection is performed between the adipose and neural tissues surrounding the IMA, followed by clipping and dividing the root of the IMA.

The surgeon applies ventral retraction to the distal edge of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), extending the dissection plane between the sigmoidal mesentery and retroperitoneum. The left-sided colon is mobilized, and posterior dissection of the rectum is continued, with the posterior side of the rectum being pushed up to extend the loose fibrous layer.

The surgeon preserves the left hypogastric nerve while identifying the S-III pelvic splanchnic nerve on the left side. An intraperitoneal loose body is visible embedded in the anterior wall of the rectum.
The video shows an intraperitoneal loose body firmly embedded in the anterior wall of the rectum, surrounded by dense pelvic fibrosis resulting from previous high-dose radiation therapy.

The surgeon continues dissection on the posterior side of the rectum, preserving the S-III and S-IV pelvic splanchnic nerves on the left side. The rectum is mobilized along the total mesorectal excision (TME) plane, with careful preservation of the S-IV nerve branch and neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon divides the hiatal ligament on the posterior side and performs dissection between the rectum and the levator ani muscle, connecting to the dissection layer created by the transanal approach. The intersphincteric dissection is extended bilaterally from the posterior right side to the left side.

On the anterior side of the rectum, the surgeon encounters severe fibrous changes in the connective tissues, making it difficult to identify a clear dissection layer due to increased fibrosis from prior radiotherapy.

On the anterior left side, the inner ring of the wound retractor is visible. The Firefly system of the DaVinci-G is being used to attempt detection of a fluorescent signal from a lighted stent, although no clear signal is detected at this stage.

The surgeon detects a bright green fluorescent signal from the urethra stent, aiding in the anterior dissection despite increased fibrosis from prior radiotherapy. The fluorescent signal provides a reference for determining the dissection line, while special care is taken to avoid injury to the urethra.

The surgeon divides the perineal body at the inferior border of the prostate, providing a final view of the anterior side from the transabdominal approach. The fluorescent signal from the lighted urethra stent assists in determining the appropriate dissection line on the anterior side.
